title: Slicing the Image
concept: Before feeding an image to a Vision Transformer, it must be chopped into a grid of non-overlapping square patches, which act as the 'words' of the image.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main role of the CLS token in a Vision Transformer?

options.text: To aggregate representation information from all other patches via self-attention.
To slice the raw image into non-overlapping grids.
To serve as a dropout regularizer.

exercises.explain: Correct! The CLS token acts as the central spokesperson, collecting context from all other patches for the final classifier.

cards.title: Chop it up
cards.body: If you feed a raw image into a transformer pixel-by-pixel, the sequence would be too long. For a 224x224 image, that's 50,176 pixels! To fix this, we chop the image into a grid of square patches, treating each patch as a single unit.

cards.title: Image Slicing
cards.body: Image slicing is the process of dividing a 2D image of size H x W into a grid of smaller, non-overlapping square patches of size P x P. These patches are then flattened into vectors to form a 1D sequence.

cards.title: Comic strip panels
cards.body: Think of an image as a comic strip. If you read it page-by-page, it's too much info at once. If you look at every individual ink dot, you get lost. But if you look panel-by-panel, you can read the story in a sequence of manageable, meaningful scenes.

cards.title: 16x16 Grid
cards.body: A standard ViT takes a 224x224 pixel image and chops it into 16x16 patches. This creates a grid of 14x14 patches, resulting in a sequence of exactly 196 patches.

cards.title: No overlap!
cards.body: You might think the patches should overlap like sliding convolutional windows to capture edges. In standard ViT, patches are strictly non-overlapping. They touch at the borders but never double-count pixels.

cards.title: Pixels vs. Patches
cards.body: Comparing sequence lengths of pixels and patches:

left.text:
224x224 = 50,176 inputs
Too long for self-attention
Extremely high memory footprint

right.text:
14x14 grid = 196 inputs
Perfect size for transformers
Computes fast and efficiently

exercises.prompt: Why do we slice images into patches in Vision Transformers?
exercises.explain: Processing pixels directly makes the sequence too long. Slicing it into patches keeps the sequence length manageable.

options.text: To reduce the input sequence length so the transformer can handle it efficiently
To remove the background noise from the image
To increase the spatial resolution of the color space
To convert the image to black-and-white format

exercises.prompt: True or false: Image patches in a standard ViT overlap with each other by default.
exercises.explain: False. Patches in standard ViTs are non-overlapping squares that cover the image like a checkerboard.
224 / 16 = 14 patches per side. 14 x 14 = 196 total patches.
exercises.sentence: If you slice a 224x224 image using a patch size of 16x16, you will get a grid of ___ patches.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following represents the flattening of a 16x16 patch with 3 color channels (RGB)?
exercises.explain: Correct! Flatting multiplies the dimensions: 16 pixels high by 16 pixels wide by 3 color channels equals a 768-element vector.

options.text: A 1D vector of length 768 (16 x 16 x 3)

exercises.prompt: If you reduce the patch size from 16x16 to 8x8 on the same size image, what happens to the sequence length?

options.text: It quadruples, because you get four times as many patches.
It is cut in half, because the patches are smaller.
It becomes zero because the patches are too small to count.

exercises.explain: Superb! Halving the dimensions of the patch squares quadruples the total count of patches. Let's see how these patches are converted into vectors next!
